name: Cepheus
description: Cepheus, in Greek mythology, was the king of Aethiopia. He is best known for being the husband of Cassiopeia and the father of Andromeda. Cassiopeia, boasting that Andromeda was more beautiful than the Nereids, angered Poseidon, who sent a sea monster, Cetus, to ravage the Aethiopian coast. To appease Poseidon, Cepheus was forced to chain Andromeda to a rock as a sacrifice to the monster. Andromeda was then rescued by the hero Perseus, who slew Cetus and married her. Cepheus, along with his wife and daughter, were later placed among the stars as constellations. While Cepheus' individual heroic feats are limited, his role in the Andromeda myth is significant. He embodies the difficult position of a ruler forced to make a terrible decision to protect his kingdom, highlighting the complexities of power and the capricious nature of the gods. His story emphasizes the importance of piety and the consequences of hubris, as well as the triumph of heroism and divine intervention. Cepheus' legacy lives on through the constellation that bears his name, a reminder of his place in the cosmic drama of Greek mythology. He represents a regal figure caught in a web of divine anger and heroic intervention, ultimately immortalized in the heavens.
subtitle: King of Aethiopia, Husband, Father, Constellation

keyMyths.title: The Sacrifice of Andromeda
keyMyths.description: To appease Poseidon's wrath, Cepheus was forced to chain his daughter Andromeda to a rock to be sacrificed to the sea monster Cetus. Perseus intervened and rescued Andromeda, saving both her and Aethiopia from destruction.

keyMyths.title: The Naming of the Constellations
keyMyths.description: After their deaths, Cepheus, Cassiopeia, and Andromeda were all placed among the stars by Poseidon or Athena, forming constellations. This immortalized their story in the night sky.

keyMyths.title: Cepheus' Lineage
keyMyths.description: Cepheus is often described as a descendant of Io, linking him to the Argive lineage. This connection reinforces his royal status and his place within the broader narrative of Greek mythology.

sources.author: Hyginus
sources.description: A collection of myths and legends about the constellations.
sources.text: A collection of myths and legends about the constellations.

sources.author: Ovid
sources.description: A narrative poem recounting tales of transformation in Greek and Roman mythology.
